channel.title: Best Mortgages For You – UK Property & Mortgage News
channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: Fears for Burnham’s impact on property investment
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property investors across the UK are reportedly evaluating how potential political changes under Andy Burnham could influence taxation policies, housing strategies and long-term investment stability in the property market.

Potential policy shifts under consideration

Market analysts suggest that investors might be paying close attention to several key areas that could see significant changes should Burnham assume greater political influence:

  • Possible reforms to property taxation structures
  • Changes to housing development regulations
  • Adjustments to private rental sector policies
  • Potential shifts in regional investment priorities

Market reactions and investor considerations

Whilst no concrete policy proposals have been confirmed, financial experts note that some investors appear to be adopting a cautious approach. Market observers suggest that:

  • Portfolio diversification strategies might be receiving renewed attention
  • Some investors could be reviewing their exposure to specific property sectors
  • Long-term investment plans may be undergoing reassessment

As the political landscape continues to evolve, property investors might find it beneficial to stay informed about potential policy developments and consider how these could influence their investment strategies. Professional financial advice could prove valuable for those navigating these uncertain waters.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property investors across the UK are reportedly evaluating how potential political changes under Andy Burnham could influence taxation policies, housing strategies and long-term investment stability in the property market.

Potential policy shifts under consideration

Market analysts suggest that investors might be paying close attention to several key areas that could see significant changes should Burnham assume greater political influence:

  • Possible reforms to property taxation structures
  • Changes to housing development regulations
  • Adjustments to private rental sector policies
  • Potential shifts in regional investment priorities

Market reactions and investor considerations

Whilst no concrete policy proposals have been confirmed, financial experts note that some investors appear to be adopting a cautious approach. Market observers suggest that:

  • Portfolio diversification strategies might be receiving renewed attention
  • Some investors could be reviewing their exposure to specific property sectors
  • Long-term investment plans may be undergoing reassessment

As the political landscape continues to evolve, property investors might find it beneficial to stay informed about potential policy developments and consider how these could influence their investment strategies. Professional financial advice could prove valuable for those navigating these uncertain waters.

item.title: Half of Brits say Brexit harmed housing market
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

New research suggests that 50% of UK residents feel Brexit has negatively impacted the country's housing market. The findings highlight widespread concerns about property affordability, housing supply shortages, and ongoing economic instability affecting the sector.

Public perception of Brexit's housing impact

A significant proportion of Britons appear to link housing market challenges to the UK's departure from the European Union. Many homeowners and prospective buyers report observing several concerning trends since the 2016 referendum, including:

  • Slower house price growth in some regions
  • Reduced construction of new homes
  • Decreased foreign investment in UK property
  • Higher costs for building materials affecting development

Whilst economists debate the precise extent of Brexit's influence, the public perception of its effect remains notably negative. This sentiment persists despite other factors like the pandemic and cost of living crisis also shaping market conditions.

Key concerns for buyers and sellers

For those navigating today's property market, Brexit-related uncertainties may add another layer of complexity to decision-making. Potential homebuyers might find themselves weighing up:

  • Mortgage affordability in a fluctuating economic climate
  • The availability of suitable properties in their price range
  • Long-term value prospects for different property types
  • Regional variations in market performance

Current homeowners considering selling may wish to research local market conditions thoroughly and seek professional valuations, as some areas have shown more resilience than others. Mortgage borrowers could benefit from reviewing their options, particularly with interest rates remaining higher than pre-Brexit levels.

As the UK property market continues to evolve, staying informed about economic trends and seeking independent financial advice may help individuals make confident property decisions amidst ongoing uncertainties.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

New research suggests that 50% of UK residents feel Brexit has negatively impacted the country's housing market. The findings highlight widespread concerns about property affordability, housing supply shortages, and ongoing economic instability affecting the sector.

Public perception of Brexit's housing impact

A significant proportion of Britons appear to link housing market challenges to the UK's departure from the European Union. Many homeowners and prospective buyers report observing several concerning trends since the 2016 referendum, including:

  • Slower house price growth in some regions
  • Reduced construction of new homes
  • Decreased foreign investment in UK property
  • Higher costs for building materials affecting development

Whilst economists debate the precise extent of Brexit's influence, the public perception of its effect remains notably negative. This sentiment persists despite other factors like the pandemic and cost of living crisis also shaping market conditions.

Key concerns for buyers and sellers

For those navigating today's property market, Brexit-related uncertainties may add another layer of complexity to decision-making. Potential homebuyers might find themselves weighing up:

  • Mortgage affordability in a fluctuating economic climate
  • The availability of suitable properties in their price range
  • Long-term value prospects for different property types
  • Regional variations in market performance

Current homeowners considering selling may wish to research local market conditions thoroughly and seek professional valuations, as some areas have shown more resilience than others. Mortgage borrowers could benefit from reviewing their options, particularly with interest rates remaining higher than pre-Brexit levels.

As the UK property market continues to evolve, staying informed about economic trends and seeking independent financial advice may help individuals make confident property decisions amidst ongoing uncertainties.

item.title: Six in ten to carry out EPC improvements
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A significant majority of UK landlords appear to be preparing to upgrade their rental properties’ energy efficiency, with recent research suggesting 60% plan to make EPC-related improvements. The average estimated spend per property stands at £11,713, indicating substantial investment in meeting evolving environmental standards for rented accommodation.

Why landlords are focusing on EPC ratings

The push towards higher energy efficiency comes as the government considers stricter EPC requirements for rental properties. Whilst current regulations demand a minimum EPC rating of E, proposals could see this raised to C by 2025 for new tenancies and extended to all rentals by 2028. Property owners making pre-emptive improvements may benefit from:

  • Future-proofing their assets against regulatory changes
  • Potentially increasing property values
  • Reducing void periods by appealing to eco-conscious tenants
  • Lowering running costs through improved energy efficiency

Common EPC improvement strategies

Landlords considering upgrades might explore various approaches to boost their properties’ energy performance. Typical improvements include:

  • Installing insulation in walls, floors and lofts
  • Upgrading to double or triple glazing
  • Replacing older boilers with modern, energy-efficient models
  • Adding smart heating controls
  • Transitioning to LED lighting throughout properties

The £11,713 average spend suggests many landlords are opting for comprehensive upgrades rather than piecemeal improvements. Those budgeting for such work may wish to research available grants or green financing options that could help offset costs.

With energy efficiency becoming an increasingly important factor in the rental market, landlords making proactive improvements could find themselves at a competitive advantage. However, as regulations continue to evolve, property owners might benefit from staying informed about potential future requirements before committing to major upgrades.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A significant majority of UK landlords appear to be preparing to upgrade their rental properties’ energy efficiency, with recent research suggesting 60% plan to make EPC-related improvements. The average estimated spend per property stands at £11,713, indicating substantial investment in meeting evolving environmental standards for rented accommodation.

Why landlords are focusing on EPC ratings

The push towards higher energy efficiency comes as the government considers stricter EPC requirements for rental properties. Whilst current regulations demand a minimum EPC rating of E, proposals could see this raised to C by 2025 for new tenancies and extended to all rentals by 2028. Property owners making pre-emptive improvements may benefit from:

  • Future-proofing their assets against regulatory changes
  • Potentially increasing property values
  • Reducing void periods by appealing to eco-conscious tenants
  • Lowering running costs through improved energy efficiency

Common EPC improvement strategies

Landlords considering upgrades might explore various approaches to boost their properties’ energy performance. Typical improvements include:

  • Installing insulation in walls, floors and lofts
  • Upgrading to double or triple glazing
  • Replacing older boilers with modern, energy-efficient models
  • Adding smart heating controls
  • Transitioning to LED lighting throughout properties

The £11,713 average spend suggests many landlords are opting for comprehensive upgrades rather than piecemeal improvements. Those budgeting for such work may wish to research available grants or green financing options that could help offset costs.

With energy efficiency becoming an increasingly important factor in the rental market, landlords making proactive improvements could find themselves at a competitive advantage. However, as regulations continue to evolve, property owners might benefit from staying informed about potential future requirements before committing to major upgrades.

item.title: Rental market remains balanced in first month of RRA
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK rental market has shown signs of stabilisation during the initial weeks following the introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act (RRA), according to recent industry observations. Estate agency Foxtons has reported a simultaneous increase in both tenant demand and property availability, suggesting a more balanced landscape for renters and landlords alike.

Market conditions show early signs of equilibrium

Early indicators from the lettings market reveal an interesting dynamic since the RRA came into force. While tenant enquiries have risen noticeably, the supply of available rental properties has also strengthened. This dual movement has helped prevent the extreme supply-demand imbalances that characterised parts of the rental market in recent years.

Industry analysts suggest several factors may be contributing to this trend:

  • Seasonal patterns typically bringing more properties to market
  • Landlords reviewing portfolios following legislative changes
  • Gradual return of some short-term lets to the long-term market

Navigating the new rental landscape

The early weeks of the RRA implementation have seen both tenants and landlords adapting to the revised regulatory framework. For renters, the increased choice may provide more negotiating power, particularly in areas where supply has risen significantly. Meanwhile, landlords might find it beneficial to review their property offerings and tenancy terms to remain competitive.

Prospective tenants could consider:

  • Comparing multiple properties before committing
  • Reviewing tenancy agreements carefully under the new rules
  • Being prepared with necessary documentation given continued demand

As the market continues to adjust to the new legislation, both renters and property investors may wish to monitor local conditions closely. The coming months will provide clearer indications of whether this initial balance represents a temporary fluctuation or a more sustained market shift.
item.content:encoded:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK rental market has shown signs of stabilisation during the initial weeks following the introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act (RRA), according to recent industry observations. Estate agency Foxtons has reported a simultaneous increase in both tenant demand and property availability, suggesting a more balanced landscape for renters and landlords alike.

Market conditions show early signs of equilibrium

Early indicators from the lettings market reveal an interesting dynamic since the RRA came into force. While tenant enquiries have risen noticeably, the supply of available rental properties has also strengthened. This dual movement has helped prevent the extreme supply-demand imbalances that characterised parts of the rental market in recent years.

Industry analysts suggest several factors may be contributing to this trend:

  • Seasonal patterns typically bringing more properties to market
  • Landlords reviewing portfolios following legislative changes
  • Gradual return of some short-term lets to the long-term market

Navigating the new rental landscape

The early weeks of the RRA implementation have seen both tenants and landlords adapting to the revised regulatory framework. For renters, the increased choice may provide more negotiating power, particularly in areas where supply has risen significantly. Meanwhile, landlords might find it beneficial to review their property offerings and tenancy terms to remain competitive.

Prospective tenants could consider:

  • Comparing multiple properties before committing
  • Reviewing tenancy agreements carefully under the new rules
  • Being prepared with necessary documentation given continued demand

As the market continues to adjust to the new legislation, both renters and property investors may wish to monitor local conditions closely. The coming months will provide clearer indications of whether this initial balance represents a temporary fluctuation or a more sustained market shift.

item.title: Manchester tops buy-to-let hitlist
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Manchester has emerged as the UK's most attractive city for buy-to-let investors, surpassing London for the first time in recent years. New research indicates that the northern powerhouse now offers stronger rental yields and more consistent tenant demand than the capital, making it a compelling proposition for property investors.

Why Manchester is leading the buy-to-let market

Several key factors have contributed to Manchester's rise to the top of buy-to-let investment rankings:

  • Higher average rental yields (typically 5-7%) compared to London's 3-4% range
  • More affordable property prices allowing for lower entry costs
  • A growing population of young professionals and students creating sustained rental demand
  • Major regeneration projects improving infrastructure and amenities
  • Strong employment growth in sectors like technology and media

Comparing regional buy-to-let hotspots

Whilst Manchester currently leads the rankings, other northern cities also present interesting opportunities for property investors. Birmingham, Liverpool and Leeds all offer competitive yields above the national average, often combined with lower purchase prices than southern locations.

London remains popular with some investors due to its global reputation and long-term capital growth potential, though the higher entry costs and tighter yields mean it may suit different investment strategies. Buy-to-let landlords might consider factors such as:

  • Local employment trends and major employers
  • Transport links and planned infrastructure improvements
  • Student populations and university expansion plans
  • Council licensing schemes and landlord requirements

As the buy-to-let landscape evolves, Manchester's combination of strong yields, growing demand and relative affordability appears to be resonating with investors. However, thorough research into local markets remains essential before making any property investment decisions.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Manchester has emerged as the UK's most attractive city for buy-to-let investors, surpassing London for the first time in recent years. New research indicates that the northern powerhouse now offers stronger rental yields and more consistent tenant demand than the capital, making it a compelling proposition for property investors.

Why Manchester is leading the buy-to-let market

Several key factors have contributed to Manchester's rise to the top of buy-to-let investment rankings:

  • Higher average rental yields (typically 5-7%) compared to London's 3-4% range
  • More affordable property prices allowing for lower entry costs
  • A growing population of young professionals and students creating sustained rental demand
  • Major regeneration projects improving infrastructure and amenities
  • Strong employment growth in sectors like technology and media

Comparing regional buy-to-let hotspots

Whilst Manchester currently leads the rankings, other northern cities also present interesting opportunities for property investors. Birmingham, Liverpool and Leeds all offer competitive yields above the national average, often combined with lower purchase prices than southern locations.

London remains popular with some investors due to its global reputation and long-term capital growth potential, though the higher entry costs and tighter yields mean it may suit different investment strategies. Buy-to-let landlords might consider factors such as:

  • Local employment trends and major employers
  • Transport links and planned infrastructure improvements
  • Student populations and university expansion plans
  • Council licensing schemes and landlord requirements

As the buy-to-let landscape evolves, Manchester's combination of strong yields, growing demand and relative affordability appears to be resonating with investors. However, thorough research into local markets remains essential before making any property investment decisions.

item.title: Where Can You Order MDF Cut to Size Online? A Buyer’s Guide from Harlow Bros
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

For homeowners and property developers undertaking renovation projects, sourcing quality MDF (medium-density fibreboard) cut to precise specifications can streamline DIY work and professional builds alike. Several reputable online timber merchants now offer this service, though selecting the right supplier requires careful consideration beyond price alone.

Key Considerations When Ordering MDF Online

When purchasing pre-cut MDF sheets online, several factors could influence the success of a project:

  • Material expertise – Suppliers with knowledgeable staff can advise on appropriate thicknesses and grades for specific applications
  • Cutting precision – Accurate measurements and clean cuts prevent material wastage and fitting issues
  • Delivery options – Reliable logistics ensure materials arrive undamaged and on schedule
  • Product range – Multiple board options allow for selection of moisture-resistant or fire-rated variants where needed

Finding Reputable Online Timber Merchants

Homeowners might consider these approaches when researching MDF suppliers:

  • Check independent review platforms for customer feedback on cutting accuracy and delivery reliability
  • Compare sample pricing for identical specifications across multiple suppliers
  • Verify whether merchants offer technical support for product selection
  • Assess delivery costs and timeframes, particularly for urgent projects

While cost remains important, the most suitable supplier typically combines competitive pricing with expert guidance and dependable service. This balanced approach can help avoid costly mistakes and project delays when working with pre-cut MDF materials.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

For homeowners and property developers undertaking renovation projects, sourcing quality MDF (medium-density fibreboard) cut to precise specifications can streamline DIY work and professional builds alike. Several reputable online timber merchants now offer this service, though selecting the right supplier requires careful consideration beyond price alone.

Key Considerations When Ordering MDF Online

When purchasing pre-cut MDF sheets online, several factors could influence the success of a project:

  • Material expertise – Suppliers with knowledgeable staff can advise on appropriate thicknesses and grades for specific applications
  • Cutting precision – Accurate measurements and clean cuts prevent material wastage and fitting issues
  • Delivery options – Reliable logistics ensure materials arrive undamaged and on schedule
  • Product range – Multiple board options allow for selection of moisture-resistant or fire-rated variants where needed

Finding Reputable Online Timber Merchants

Homeowners might consider these approaches when researching MDF suppliers:

  • Check independent review platforms for customer feedback on cutting accuracy and delivery reliability
  • Compare sample pricing for identical specifications across multiple suppliers
  • Verify whether merchants offer technical support for product selection
  • Assess delivery costs and timeframes, particularly for urgent projects

While cost remains important, the most suitable supplier typically combines competitive pricing with expert guidance and dependable service. This balanced approach can help avoid costly mistakes and project delays when working with pre-cut MDF materials.

item.title: The £3bn shake-up few agents and landlords saw coming
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Proposed Deposit Protection Changes Could Impact £3bn in Tenant Funds

Government proposals to reform tenancy deposit protection in England and Wales may affect over £3bn held in insured schemes, according to analysis by The Letting Partnership. The plans could require all deposits to be held in custodial arrangements, replacing the current system where landlords and letting agents may opt for insured protection models.

Current figures suggest approximately 4.7 million deposits are protected across both nations, with 45.6% (2.1 million) using insured schemes. While custodial schemes account for a slight majority (54.4%) of protected deposits, insured arrangements represent the larger share of monetary value – holding £3bn compared to £2.5bn in custodial schemes.

Industry Debates Potential Transition Challenges

The proposals have sparked discussion across the lettings sector regarding the future of deposit protection. Key considerations include:

  • The operational impact on landlords and agents currently using insured schemes
  • How existing insured deposits might transition to custodial protection
  • Whether a phased approach would see new tenancies using custodial schemes while existing arrangements run their course

Chris Mason, COO of The Letting Partnership, noted: "The debate often focuses on who should hold deposits, but the fundamental issue concerns transparency. Neither schemes, underwriters nor government currently have full visibility of the cash position behind these liabilities at any given time."

Mason added that while the government appears to be addressing genuine concerns about tenant money transparency, the industry could face a complex transition lasting several years if changes are implemented. This might require agents to operate both protection models simultaneously during the switchover period.

As consultations continue, stakeholders across the property sector will be monitoring developments closely, particularly regarding how any reforms might balance consumer protection with practical implementation challenges.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

Proposed Deposit Protection Changes Could Impact £3bn in Tenant Funds

Government proposals to reform tenancy deposit protection in England and Wales may affect over £3bn held in insured schemes, according to analysis by The Letting Partnership. The plans could require all deposits to be held in custodial arrangements, replacing the current system where landlords and letting agents may opt for insured protection models.

Current figures suggest approximately 4.7 million deposits are protected across both nations, with 45.6% (2.1 million) using insured schemes. While custodial schemes account for a slight majority (54.4%) of protected deposits, insured arrangements represent the larger share of monetary value – holding £3bn compared to £2.5bn in custodial schemes.

Industry Debates Potential Transition Challenges

The proposals have sparked discussion across the lettings sector regarding the future of deposit protection. Key considerations include:

  • The operational impact on landlords and agents currently using insured schemes
  • How existing insured deposits might transition to custodial protection
  • Whether a phased approach would see new tenancies using custodial schemes while existing arrangements run their course

Chris Mason, COO of The Letting Partnership, noted: "The debate often focuses on who should hold deposits, but the fundamental issue concerns transparency. Neither schemes, underwriters nor government currently have full visibility of the cash position behind these liabilities at any given time."

Mason added that while the government appears to be addressing genuine concerns about tenant money transparency, the industry could face a complex transition lasting several years if changes are implemented. This might require agents to operate both protection models simultaneously during the switchover period.

As consultations continue, stakeholders across the property sector will be monitoring developments closely, particularly regarding how any reforms might balance consumer protection with practical implementation challenges.

item.title: Vast majority of tenants still can’t find what they’re looking for
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Rental Market Challenges Persist Despite Tenant Protections

New research highlights a growing divide between tenant rights and rental market realities. Whilst 89% of landlords acknowledge that recent legislation, including the Renters’ Rights Act, has strengthened tenants’ position, many renters still struggle to find suitable accommodation. The LRG Spring 2026 Lettings Report reveals that 78% of tenants searching for a property in the past two years faced fewer options than expected, with affordability and limited availability being key barriers.

The figures paint a challenging picture for renters. A quarter of tenants wished to move in the last year but were unable to, with reasons ranging from financial constraints to a lack of suitable properties. Only 5% successfully relocated during this period. Rising rents add further pressure, with the ONS reporting a 3.5% annual increase, pushing the average monthly private rent to £1,381 in April 2026.

Supply Shortages and Homeownership Hurdles

The rental market’s supply crisis shows little sign of easing. Data from Rightmove indicates available rental stock remains 33% lower than a decade ago, whilst Zoopla reports levels still 20-30% below pre-pandemic figures across all regions. For those considering homeownership as an alternative, the outlook remains difficult. A third of tenants admitted they would still be unable to buy even if house prices fell significantly in their area, with just 16% stating they would act immediately.

Allison Thompson, chief lettings officer at Leaders (part of LRG), noted: "Landlords and tenants are describing the same market from different perspectives. Landlords feel the impact of legislative changes, whilst tenants face the daily reality of limited choice and rising costs."

Whilst regulatory reforms have strengthened tenant protections, the fundamental issue of supply and affordability continues to dominate the rental market. For many, the search for a suitable home remains an uphill battle.
item.content:encoded:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Rental Market Challenges Persist Despite Tenant Protections

New research highlights a growing divide between tenant rights and rental market realities. Whilst 89% of landlords acknowledge that recent legislation, including the Renters’ Rights Act, has strengthened tenants’ position, many renters still struggle to find suitable accommodation. The LRG Spring 2026 Lettings Report reveals that 78% of tenants searching for a property in the past two years faced fewer options than expected, with affordability and limited availability being key barriers.

The figures paint a challenging picture for renters. A quarter of tenants wished to move in the last year but were unable to, with reasons ranging from financial constraints to a lack of suitable properties. Only 5% successfully relocated during this period. Rising rents add further pressure, with the ONS reporting a 3.5% annual increase, pushing the average monthly private rent to £1,381 in April 2026.

Supply Shortages and Homeownership Hurdles

The rental market’s supply crisis shows little sign of easing. Data from Rightmove indicates available rental stock remains 33% lower than a decade ago, whilst Zoopla reports levels still 20-30% below pre-pandemic figures across all regions. For those considering homeownership as an alternative, the outlook remains difficult. A third of tenants admitted they would still be unable to buy even if house prices fell significantly in their area, with just 16% stating they would act immediately.

Allison Thompson, chief lettings officer at Leaders (part of LRG), noted: "Landlords and tenants are describing the same market from different perspectives. Landlords feel the impact of legislative changes, whilst tenants face the daily reality of limited choice and rising costs."

Whilst regulatory reforms have strengthened tenant protections, the fundamental issue of supply and affordability continues to dominate the rental market. For many, the search for a suitable home remains an uphill battle.

item.title: Setting rents just got more complicated – are you up to speed with the changes?
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters’ Rights Act has introduced significant changes to how rents are set and managed in the private rented sector, leaving letting agents with new responsibilities and challenges. With the legislation now in effect, agents are required to adapt their processes to ensure compliance, particularly when it comes to evidencing market rents and handling tenant requests. To help navigate these changes, industry experts are offering guidance, including a recent webinar hosted by Rightmove.

Understanding the New Rent-Setting Requirements

One of the key aspects of the Renters’ Rights Act is the requirement for letting agents to provide clear evidence when setting or reviewing rents. This means agents must now document their decisions thoroughly, ensuring they can justify rental amounts based on market conditions. The legislation aims to promote transparency and fairness, but it also means agents need to be meticulous in their record-keeping.

For instance, if a tenant challenges a rent increase, agents must be able to demonstrate how the new rent aligns with comparable properties in the area. This could involve gathering data on local rental trends, analysing similar listings, and maintaining detailed records of their findings. Failure to comply could result in disputes being escalated to the First-tier Tribunal, where agents will need to present robust evidence to support their case.

Practical Guidance for Agents

To assist letting agents in navigating these changes, Rightmove recently hosted a webinar featuring industry consultant David d’Orton-Gibson. The session provided practical advice on several key areas, including how to handle tenant requests to change rent payment dates and how to prepare for potential tribunal challenges. The webinar emphasised the importance of consistency and clarity in rent-setting procedures, helping agents to approach their responsibilities with confidence.

Jason Charles, head of education at Rightmove, highlighted the need for agents to stay informed and adaptable. He stated, “The Renters’ Rights Act has fundamentally altered how rents are managed, and agents must feel equipped to meet these new expectations. Our webinar was designed to provide actionable insights, ensuring agents can operate with clarity and consistency in this evolving landscape.”

Preparing for the Future

As the industry continues to adapt to the Renters’ Rights Act, many letting agents are reviewing their internal processes to ensure compliance. This includes updating procedures for documenting market rents, training staff on the new requirements, and implementing systems to handle tenant requests efficiently. By taking proactive steps now, agents can minimise the risk of disputes and ensure smoother operations moving forward.

For those seeking further guidance, resources such as webinars and industry consultations can be invaluable. Staying informed and seeking expert advice could help agents navigate the complexities of the new legislation whilst maintaining positive relationships with tenants.

The Renters’ Rights Act represents a significant shift in the private rented sector, placing greater emphasis on transparency and fairness. Letting agents who take the time to understand and implement the new requirements will be better positioned to succeed in this changing landscape.
item.content:encoded:
By Bambang Setiawan

The Renters’ Rights Act has introduced significant changes to how rents are set and managed in the private rented sector, leaving letting agents with new responsibilities and challenges. With the legislation now in effect, agents are required to adapt their processes to ensure compliance, particularly when it comes to evidencing market rents and handling tenant requests. To help navigate these changes, industry experts are offering guidance, including a recent webinar hosted by Rightmove.

Understanding the New Rent-Setting Requirements

One of the key aspects of the Renters’ Rights Act is the requirement for letting agents to provide clear evidence when setting or reviewing rents. This means agents must now document their decisions thoroughly, ensuring they can justify rental amounts based on market conditions. The legislation aims to promote transparency and fairness, but it also means agents need to be meticulous in their record-keeping.

For instance, if a tenant challenges a rent increase, agents must be able to demonstrate how the new rent aligns with comparable properties in the area. This could involve gathering data on local rental trends, analysing similar listings, and maintaining detailed records of their findings. Failure to comply could result in disputes being escalated to the First-tier Tribunal, where agents will need to present robust evidence to support their case.

Practical Guidance for Agents

To assist letting agents in navigating these changes, Rightmove recently hosted a webinar featuring industry consultant David d’Orton-Gibson.
